date: 2026-06-14
title: Friedrich Nietzsche - Biographical Facts
source: https://plato.stanford.edu/ENTRIES/nietzsche-life-works/, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Friedrich_Wilhelm_Nietzsche, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Friedrich_Nietzsche_bibliography
---

# Friedrich Nietzsche - Biographical Facts

## Identity Facts

- **Full name:** Friedrich Wilhelm Nietzsche
- **Birth:** 15 October 1844, Röcken bei Lützen, Province of Saxony, Prussia
- **Death:** 25 August 1900, Weimar, Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach, German Empire (age 55)
- **Cause of death:** Pneumonia combined with stroke
- **Burial:** Röcken Churchyard, beside his father
- **Nationality:** Prussian (birth); stateless from 1869 (renounced Prussian citizenship; never acquired Swiss citizenship)
- **Languages:** German (native), Latin, Greek (classical education), French, Italian (reading/correspondence)
- **Education:** Schulpforta boarding school (1858-1864); University of Bonn (1864-1865, theology and philology); University of Leipzig (1865-1869, philology); Honorary doctorate from Leipzig (1869)
- **Occupation:** Classical philologist, philosopher, writer, cultural critic, composer
- **Academic title:** Professor of Classical Philology, University of Basel (1869-1879)
- **Residences:** Röcken (1844-1849); Naumburg (1849-1858, and intermittently); Schulpforta (1858-1864); Bonn (1864-1865); Leipzig (1865-1869); Basel, Switzerland (1869-1879); Nomadic period (1880-1889): Nice, Sils-Maria, Turin, Genoa, Venice, Rome, Rapallo, Messina, Florence, Recoaro; Naumburg (1890-1897, under mother's care); Weimar (1897-1900, Villa Silberblick, under sister's care)

## Family and Relationships

| Person | Relationship | Period | Key facts |
|---|---|---|---|
| Karl Ludwig Nietzsche (1813-1849) | Father | 1844-1849 | Lutheran pastor at Röcken. Named after Prussian King Friedrich Wilhelm IV who appointed him. Died of brain ailment on 30 July 1849 when Friedrich was 4. Friedrich deeply affected by loss. |
| Franziska Nietzsche, née Oehler (1826-1897) | Mother | 1844-1897 | Raised Friedrich after husband's death. Moved family to Naumburg. Cared for Friedrich 1890-1897 after his collapse. Died 1897. Devoted but relationship strained in later years over his anti-Christian writings. |
| Ludwig Joseph Nietzsche (1848-1850) | Brother | 1848-1850 | Younger brother. Died 4 January 1850, six months after father. Friedrich traumatized by both deaths in quick succession. |
| Therese Elisabeth Alexandra Nietzsche (1846-1935) | Sister | 1846-1900 | Two years younger. Intensely close but conflicted relationship. Married Bernhard Förster 1885. Went to Paraguay 1886-1893. Returned after husband's suicide. Took control of Nietzsche's literary estate and care 1893-1900. Distorted his philosophy for nationalist/anti-Semitic purposes. Founded Nietzsche Archive in Weimar. |
| Erdmuthe Dorothea Nietzsche, née Krause (1778-1856) | Paternal grandmother | 1844-1856 | Lived with family in Naumburg after father's death. Helped raise Friedrich. |
| Auguste Nietzsche (d. 1855) | Paternal aunt | 1844-1855 | Lived with family in Naumburg. |
| Rosalie Nietzsche (d. 1867) | Paternal aunt | 1844-1867 | Lived with family in Naumburg. |
| Friedrich August Ludwig Nietzsche (1756-1826) | Paternal grandfather | Pre-1844 | Protestant scholar. Wrote book affirming everlasting survival of Christianity (1796). Lutheran minister. |
| David Ernst Oehler (1787-1859) | Maternal grandfather | 1844-1859 | From Zeitz. |
| Richard Wagner (1813-1883) | Friend/mentor, then rival | 1868-1883 | Met November 1868 in Leipzig. Quasi-familial bond; Wagner same age as Nietzsche's father would have been. Nietzsche called friendship "greatest achievement" of life (1869). Frequent visits to Tribschen and Bayreuth. Break in late 1870s over Wagner's Christianity and nationalism. Nietzsche devastated by the rupture. Still said days with Wagner were best of his life (1882). Wagner died 1883; Nietzsche continued wrestling with his legacy until collapse. |
| Cosima Wagner (1837-1930) | Friend (through Wagner) | 1869-1878 | Wife of Richard Wagner. Nietzsche sent her one of his "madness letters" in January 1889. Relationship ended with the break from Wagner. |
| Franz Overbeck (1837-1905) | Close friend | 1870-1900 | Church historian at Basel. Shared a house for five years. Lifelong correspondent. Rushed to Turin January 1889 to rescue Nietzsche after collapse. One of very few who remained loyal throughout. |
| Jacob Burckhardt (1818-1897) | Intellectual mentor | 1869-1889 | Historian of culture at Basel. Nietzsche attended his lectures. Received one of the "madness letters" in January 1889. Respected but never intimate relationship. |
| Paul Deussen (1845-1919) | Lifelong friend | 1858-1889 | Met at Schulpforta; confirmed together 1861. Became Orientalist and philosopher. Founded Schopenhauer Society 1911. Loyal friend throughout. |
| Erwin Rohde (1845-1898) | Close friend | 1865-c.1887 | Fellow philology student at Leipzig. Extensive correspondence. Defended Birth of Tragedy publicly. Friendship cooled in later years. |
| Paul Rée (1849-1901) | Close friend, then rival | 1873-1882 | Philosopher. Met 1873. Close companion in Sorrento 1876. Wrote On the Origin of Moral Feelings. Friendship destroyed by Lou Salomé affair 1882; Rée and Salomé left together for Berlin. Died 1901 (fell from cliff, possible suicide). |
| Lou von Salomé (1861-1937) | Unrequited love | 1882 | Met in Rome, spring 1882. Russian intellectual, 21 years old. Nietzsche proposed marriage (rejected). Intellectual triangle with Rée. She left with Rée. Later wrote insightful book on Nietzsche (1894). Became associate of Freud (1911). Nietzsche bitter about rejection for years. |
| Peter Gast / Johann Heinrich Köselitz (1854-1918) | Friend, secretary, disciple | 1875-1900 | Composer and writer. Nietzsche's most faithful helper - copied manuscripts, corrected proofs. Gave funeral oration. Sometimes sycophantic. |
| Heinrich Romundt | Friend | 1872-c.1876 | Philosopher at Basel. Shared lodgings with Nietzsche and Overbeck. Friendship ended when Romundt converted to Catholicism. |
| Bernhard Förster (1843-1889) | Brother-in-law | 1885-1889 | Anti-Semitic agitator. Married Elisabeth 1885. Founded "Nueva Germania" colony in Paraguay. Nietzsche despised his anti-Semitism. Committed suicide 1889. |
| Friedrich Wilhelm Ritschl (1806-1876) | Academic mentor | 1864-1869 | Professor of classical philology. Nietzsche followed him from Bonn to Leipzig. Recommended Nietzsche for Basel position. Called Nietzsche the most gifted student he had seen in 39 years. |
| Arthur Schopenhauer (1788-1860) | Intellectual idol | 1865 onward | Never met (Schopenhauer died 1860). Discovered The World as Will and Representation by chance in 1865. Profound early influence, later partially rejected. |
| Ulrich von Wilamowitz-Möllendorff (1848-1931) | Rival/antagonist | 1872 onward | Fellow Schulpforta alumnus. Published devastating critique of Birth of Tragedy (1872). Called Nietzsche "a disgrace to Schulpforta." Became leading German philologist. |
| Georg Brandes (1842-1927) | Intellectual champion | 1888 | Danish critic. First to lecture publicly on Nietzsche's philosophy (Copenhagen, 1888). Correspondence with Nietzsche. Recommended he read Kierkegaard. |
| Meta von Salis (1855-1929) | Friend and patron | 1884-1900 | Swiss feminist, historian. Financial supporter. Visited regularly during nomadic years. |
| Carl von Gersdorff (1844-1904) | School friend | 1858-c.1876 | Met at Schulpforta. Close friend during Basel years. Friendship faded. |
| Malwida von Meysenbug (1816-1903) | Maternal friend | 1872-c.1882 | Writer and feminist. Hosted Nietzsche at Sorrento 1876-1877. Introduced him to Lou Salomé. Relationship cooled after Salomé affair. |

## Career Timeline

| Year | Event |
|---|---|
| 1844 | Born 15 October in Röcken bei Lützen, Province of Saxony, Prussia |
| 1849 | Father Karl Ludwig dies of brain ailment (30 July). Family left without male head. |
| 1850 | Brother Ludwig Joseph dies (4 January). Family moves to Naumburg. |
| 1854 | Attends Domgymnasium in Naumburg |
| 1858 | Enters Schulpforta boarding school on scholarship. Founds "Germania" music and literature club. |
| 1860 | Earliest surviving musical compositions |
| 1861 | Confirmed alongside Paul Deussen. Writes first philosophical essays (Fate and History; Freedom of Will and Fate, 1862). |
| 1864 | Graduates from Schulpforta. Enters University of Bonn studying theology and philology. |
| 1865 | Transfers to University of Leipzig following Ritschl. Abandons theology. Discovers Schopenhauer's The World as Will and Representation. Loss of Christian faith. |
| 1866 | Reads F.A. Lange's History of Materialism. First philological publications on Theognis and Simonides. |
| 1867 | Enters required Prussian military service (equestrian field artillery). Serious chest injury while mounting horse; put on sick leave. |
| 1868 | Returns to Leipzig. Meets Richard Wagner (November) at home of Hermann Brockhaus. |
| 1869 | Offered professorship at University of Basel at age 24 (without completed doctorate). Honorary doctorate from Leipzig. Begins teaching May 1869. Renounces Prussian citizenship. First visits to Wagner at Tribschen. |
| 1870 | Promoted to full professor at Basel. Serves as medical orderly in Franco-Prussian War (August-October). Contracts diphtheria and dysentery at siege of Metz. Returns to Basel in poor health. |
| 1871 | Health problems intensify. Applies unsuccessfully for philosophy chair at Basel. |
| 1872 | Publishes The Birth of Tragedy (January). Devastating review by Wilamowitz-Möllendorff damages academic reputation. Student enrollments drop sharply. Delivers lectures "On the Future of Our Educational Institutions." |
| 1873 | Meets Paul Rée. Publishes first two Untimely Meditations (David Strauss; History for Life). Writes "On Truth and Lies in a Nonmoral Sense" (unpublished). |
| 1874 | Publishes third Untimely Meditation (Schopenhauer as Educator). Health continuing to deteriorate - migraines, vision problems. |
| 1875 | Meets Peter Gast (Heinrich Köselitz). |
| 1876 | Publishes fourth Untimely Meditation (Richard Wagner in Bayreuth). Attends first Bayreuth Festival (August); deeply disappointed by commercial atmosphere. Autumn in Sorrento with Rée and Malwida von Meysenbug. Beginning of break with Wagner. |
| 1877 | Returns to Basel. Growing estrangement from Wagner. |
| 1878 | Publishes Human, All Too Human. Sends copy to Wagner; Wagner sends Parsifal libretto in return. Definitive break with Wagner. |
| 1879 | Publishes Mixed Opinions and Maxims. Health at its worst - 118 days of severe migraine attacks in one year. Resigns professorship at Basel (June) with pension. End of academic career after 10 years. |
| 1880 | Publishes The Wanderer and His Shadow. Begins nomadic wandering existence: Riva, Venice, Marienbad, Naumburg, Stresa, Genoa. |
| 1881 | Publishes Daybreak. Discovers Sils-Maria (Engadin) in summer - will return every summer until 1888. Conceives idea of eternal recurrence (August, at Surlej boulder near Silvaplana). |
| 1882 | Publishes The Gay Science. Meets Lou von Salomé in Rome (spring). Proposes marriage (rejected). Summer in Tautenburg with Lou and Elisabeth. Lou and Rée depart for Berlin (autumn). Deep depression and isolation. Considers suicide. |
| 1883 | Wagner dies (13 February) - Nietzsche deeply affected despite estrangement. Writes Zarathustra Part I in 10 days (Rapallo, January-February). Writes Part II (Sils-Maria, summer). Quarrel with Elisabeth over her engagement to anti-Semite Bernhard Förster. |
| 1884 | Writes Zarathustra Part III (Nice, January). Writes Part IV (Mentone and Nice, autumn-winter). |
| 1885 | Zarathustra Part IV printed privately (40 copies). Elisabeth marries Förster; departs for Paraguay. Nietzsche feels increasingly isolated. |
| 1886 | Publishes Beyond Good and Evil (at own expense). Reissues earlier works with new prefaces. |
| 1887 | Publishes On the Genealogy of Morality. Second edition of Daybreak and Gay Science (with new fifth book). |
| 1888 | Final productive year - unprecedented creative burst. Publishes The Case of Wagner. Completes Twilight of the Idols, The Antichrist, Ecce Homo, Nietzsche contra Wagner, Dionysian Dithyrambs. Georg Brandes lectures on Nietzsche in Copenhagen (spring) - first public academic recognition. Moves to Turin (April); finds the city agreeable. Signs of grandiosity and euphoria in letters by December. |
| 1889 | Mental collapse in Turin (3 January). Sends "madness letters" to friends and public figures. Franz Overbeck travels to Turin, brings Nietzsche to Basel psychiatric clinic (10 January). Transferred to Jena asylum under Otto Binswanger (17 January). Diagnosed with dementia paralytica (general paresis). Never regains mental faculties. |
| 1890 | Mother Franziska takes him from Jena asylum to family home in Naumburg (March). Lives under her care. Progressively deteriorating. |
| 1893 | Elisabeth returns from Paraguay after Bernhard Förster's suicide (1889). Begins taking control of literary estate. |
| 1894 | Elisabeth founds the Nietzsche Archive in Naumburg. |
| 1895 | The Antichrist and Nietzsche contra Wagner published for first time (by Elisabeth). Fame growing rapidly while he is incapacitated. |
| 1897 | Mother Franziska dies (April). Elisabeth moves Nietzsche and archive to Villa Silberblick, Weimar. |
| 1898 | Suffers at least one stroke. Partial paralysis. |
| 1899 | Further stroke(s). Unable to speak or walk. Left-side hemiplegia. |
| 1900 | Contracts pneumonia mid-August. Final stroke night of 24-25 August. Dies approximately noon, 25 August, at Villa Silberblick, Weimar. Buried beside father at church in Röcken. Peter Gast delivers funeral oration. |

| Morgenröte (Daybreak/The Dawn) | 1881 | Book |

| Die fröhliche Wissenschaft (The Gay Science) | 1882 (expanded 1887) | Book |
| Also sprach Zarathustra, Parts I-III (Thus Spoke Zarathustra) | 1883-1885 | Book |
| Also sprach Zarathustra, Part IV | 1885 | Book (private printing, 40 copies) |
| Jenseits von Gut und Böse (Beyond Good and Evil) | 1886 | Book |
| Zur Genealogie der Moral (On the Genealogy of Morality) | 1887 | Book |
| Der Fall Wagner (The Case of Wagner) | 1888 | Book |
| Götzen-Dämmerung (Twilight of the Idols) | 1889 (written 1888) | Book |
| Der Antichrist (The Antichrist) | 1895 (written 1888) | Book |
| Ecce Homo | 1908 (written 1888) | Autobiography |
| Nietzsche contra Wagner | 1895 (written 1888) | Book |
| Dionysos-Dithyramben (Dionysian Dithyrambs) | 1892 (written 1888) | Poetry collection |

## Events Witnessed

| Event | Year | Their reaction (if documented) |
|---|---|---|
| Revolutions of 1848 (aftermath) | 1848 | Too young to remember directly; born into post-revolutionary Prussian conservatism |
| Death of father Karl Ludwig | 1849 | Traumatic childhood loss; recurring theme in self-understanding |
| Crimean War | 1853-1856 | No documented reaction (child) |
| Italian War of Independence | 1859 | No documented reaction (age 14, at school) |
| American Civil War | 1861-1865 | No documented reaction |
| Bismarck's wars of German unification (Danish War, Austro-Prussian War) | 1864, 1866 | Contemporary awareness; later expressed contempt for Bismarck's Realpolitik |
| Franco-Prussian War | 1870-1871 | Served as medical orderly (August-October 1870). Witnessed siege of Metz. Traumatized by combat injuries he treated. Contracted diphtheria and dysentery. Shaped his views on suffering and culture. |
| German unification / founding of German Empire | 1871 | Deeply skeptical. Feared military victory would destroy German culture. Warned against conflating military power with cultural achievement. |
| Paris Commune | 1871 | Alarmed. Reportedly feared for cultural treasures. Saw it as sign of coming cultural upheaval. |
| First Bayreuth Festival | 1876 | Attended. Profoundly disappointed by commercialism, nationalism, and social spectacle. Triggered break with Wagner. |
| Publication of Darwin's works / Darwinism debates | 1860s-1880s | Anti-Darwinian. Rejected natural selection as explanation of human culture. Called Darwin "mediocre." |
| Rise of socialism and labor movements in Germany | 1870s-1880s | Hostile. Viewed socialism as herd morality, resentment of the weak against the strong. |
| Anti-Semitic movement in Germany | 1870s-1880s | Vehemently opposed. Broke with Wagner partly over anti-Semitism. Despised brother-in-law Förster's anti-Semitic colony. Called anti-Semites "failures" and "aborted." |
| Wagner's Parsifal premiere | 1882 | Disgusted by its Christian themes. Saw it as Wagner's capitulation to Christianity. A factor in their permanent estrangement. |
| Death of Richard Wagner | 1883 | Deeply affected despite years of estrangement. Said "Wagner was by far the fullest human being I have known." Wrote Zarathustra Part I shortly after. |
| Bismarck's Anti-Socialist Laws | 1878-1890 | Critical of Bismarck's statecraft generally. Called the Reich a triumph of "Bildungsphilister" (cultural philistines). |
| Colonialism / European imperial expansion | 1880s | Critical of nationalism and colonialism. Advocated "good Europeans" over national chauvinists. |
| Elisabeth and Förster depart for Nueva Germania (Paraguay) | 1886 | Scathing. Wrote anti-Semitism was "a matter for failures" and mocked the colonial venture. |
| Georg Brandes lectures on Nietzsche in Copenhagen | 1888 | Elated. First significant public recognition. Corresponded enthusiastically with Brandes. |
| Bernhard Förster's suicide in Paraguay | 1889 | Occurred same year as Nietzsche's collapse. He was already incapacitated and likely unaware. |

## Current Situation (Final Years and Death)

Friedrich Nietzsche suffered a complete mental collapse on 3 January 1889 in Turin, Italy, at age 44. According to often-repeated accounts, he witnessed a horse being flogged in the Piazza Carlo Alberto, threw his arms around the horse's neck, and collapsed. In the days following, he sent bizarre, grandiose letters ("madness letters") to friends including Burckhardt, Overbeck, Cosima Wagner, and various political figures, signing himself "Dionysus" or "The Crucified."

His friend Franz Overbeck traveled to Turin and brought Nietzsche to the psychiatric clinic in Basel (10 January 1889), where he stayed one week. He was then transferred to the Binswanger clinic (psychiatric asylum) in Jena (17 January 1889), where he was diagnosed with dementia paralytica (general paresis). He remained there until March 1890.

In March 1890, his mother Franziska removed him from the asylum and took him to the family home in Naumburg, where she cared for him for seven years. He was largely uncommunicative, recognizing visitors occasionally but unable to sustain conversation or produce coherent thought. He would sometimes play fragments on the piano.

After Franziska's death in April 1897, his sister Elisabeth assumed full control. She moved Nietzsche to the rented Villa Silberblick in Weimar, which also housed the Nietzsche Archive. Elisabeth received visitors who wished to observe the now-famous philosopher, essentially displaying him. Rudolf Steiner visited in 1895 and attempted to tutor Elisabeth in philosophy but abandoned the effort, declaring it "impossible to teach her anything about philosophy."

In 1898-1899, Nietzsche suffered at least two strokes causing partial paralysis (left-side hemiplegia). He became unable to walk or speak. He was bedridden and totally dependent on caregivers.

In mid-August 1900, he contracted pneumonia. He suffered a final stroke during the night of 24-25 August. Friedrich Nietzsche died at approximately noon on 25 August 1900 at Villa Silberblick, Weimar, at age 55. No autopsy was performed.

His body was transported to Röcken bei Lützen and buried beside his father at the village church. Peter Gast delivered the funeral oration, proclaiming: "Holy be your name to all future generations!"

The cause of his 1889 collapse remains debated. Contemporary doctors diagnosed tertiary syphilis. Modern medical historians have proposed alternative diagnoses: frontotemporal dementia, CADASIL syndrome (hereditary stroke disorder), brain tumor (retro-orbital meningioma), or other vascular/neurological conditions. His father also died young of a "brain ailment," suggesting possible hereditary component.
